text: The benefits of a low anterior abdominal incision have been previously demonstrated, including enhanced postoperative recovery and reduced postoperative pain. The reduced risk of respiratory-related complications can be achieved due to minimal disruptions on accessory respiratory muscles.

Within the domains of robotic urological surgery, there has been limited evidence of using the incision for robotic retroperitoneal partial nephrectomy due to concerns surrounding distance to the kidney and the limited working space within the retroperitoneum.

The purpose-built SPA robotic platform possess two prominent features, including the narrow profile and double articulating instruments. Both features allow for improvement of durability, especially in a small surgical working space which provide the opportunity for surgeons to regionalize surgery to the relevant anatomy.

This video presents an application of the low anterior abdominal incision for a retroperitoneal robotic partial nephrectomy on a sixty nine year old man with asymptomatic left upper pole renal mass measuring two point nine centimeters. The patient had a previous history of transperitoneal robotic simple prostatectomy.
The patient is positioned in a lateral position on the operating table, which is slightly flexed, following the induction of general anesthesia.

A 3.5 cm incision is made approximately 3 cm superior to the pubic tubercle, lateral to the midline. The surgeon incises the external oblique fascia and performs blunt dissections to separate the underlying muscles.
The surgeon uses their finger to gently push the peritoneum away, creating additional space. A purpose-built SP access kit is then inserted through the incision site.
The single-port robotic system is docked at an acute angle, aligning towards the target anatomy for the procedure.
Upon entry into the retroperitoneum, the psoas major muscle is clearly visualized. The surgeon performs dissections to expose the ureter, which is visible crossing over the iliac artery.
The surgeon continues dissection along the ureter, moving proximally towards the left kidney, with the ureter visibly being separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon opens the rotous fascia and continues dissection towards the renal hilum, meticulously exposing the hilar structures.
The surgical field reveals several critical structures, including the proximal ureter, two renal arteries, and one renal vein, clearly visualized within the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon continues dissection to expose the upper pole renal mass within the gyrotous fascia, performing minimal fat excision to reveal the mass.
After achieving adequate exposure, the surgeon uses an interpretive ultrasound to identify and mark the relevant tumor within the surgical field.
The surgeon places purpose-built robotic bulldog clamps on the two renal arteries and the single renal vein, securing these critical vascular structures.
The surgeon excises the upper pole tumor under ischemia, ensuring good hemostasis throughout the procedure.
The surgeon performs rhinorafi in two layers using V-lock sutures, securing them with hemo-lock clips through a sliding cleave technique.
After completing the rhinorafi, the surgeon removes the three robotic bulldog clamps from the renal arteries and vein, marking the end of a twenty-five-minute ischemia period.
The surgeon confirms good hemostasis and absence of urine leak before placing the excised tumor into an endocatch bag. The bag is then removed through the single incision.
The robotic system is undocked, and the surgical port is removed along with the intact specimen. The surgeon performs routine closure by layers, followed by skin closure.
The procedure took two hours and forty-eight minutes to complete without any need for conversion nor interoperative complication. The estimated interoperative blood loss was fifteen mils.

Postoperatively, the patient reported minimal pain and was discharged on the same day. Final histopathology of the specimen identified a Tier Ia clear-cell renal cell carcinoma and negative surgical margin was achieved.
The patient was reviewed one week following his surgery where he remained pain-free, and examination demonstrated good healing of the left lower abdominal incision site.

Our initial series consists of four patients with a median age of sixty-eight years and a median BMI of thirty-five point four. Three patients had previous abdominal surgery, one of which had more than five previous surgeries.
There were no interpretive and postoperative complications in our series. The median ischemia time was twenty-four minutes and negative surgical margin was achieved in all four cases.
Our initial series demonstrated the favourable perioperative outcomes with all patients being discharged within twenty-four hours and with none requiring opioid analgesia on discharge.

Our initial experience demonstrated that robotic retroperitoneal partial nephrectomy for any tumor location can be safely performed through a low anterior abdominal incision using the purpose-built SP platform. Perioperative outcomes in our series were favorable with reduced length of stay and postoperative analgesia requirement while maintaining favorable oncological and functional outcomes.
